  # Data import & wrangling

  # Static visualisation

  ggridges, ggrepel, ggthemes,
  viridis, RColorBrewer, treemapify,

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
2.2 Importing Data
The dataset is loaded from an Excel file using read_excel() from the readxl package. Four derived time variables are added for temporal analysis.

superstore <- read_excel("data/Superstore.xls")
# Add derived time variables for temporal analysis
superstore <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year      = year(`Order Date`),
    Month     = month(`Order Date`, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE),
    YearMonth = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month"),
    Quarter   = paste0("Q", quarter(`Order Date`)),
    ShipDays  = as.numeric(difftime(`Ship Date`, `Order Date`, units = "days"))
  )

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
4.1 By Category


Show Code
category_metrics <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit   = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales    = sum(Sales),
    Avg_Profit     = mean(Profit),
    Median_Profit  = median(Profit),
    Profit_Margin  = Total_Profit / Total_Sales * 100,
    Count          = n(),
    Pct_Profitable = mean(Profit > 0) * 100,
    .groups        = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

# Panel A: Boxplot profit distribution
p_cat_box <- ggplot(superstore,
                    aes(x = reorder(Category, Profit, FUN = median),
                        y = Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.75, outlier.alpha = 0.25, outlier.size = 1,
               outlier.color = "grey60") +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 4.5,
               fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Distribution by Category",
    subtitle = "Diamond = mean  |  Box = IQR  |  Whiskers = 1.5×IQR  |  Red dashed = break-even",
    x = NULL, y = "Profit per Transaction"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"), legend.position = "none")

# Panel B: Total profit bar
p_cat_total <- ggplot(category_metrics,
                      aes(x = reorder(Category, Total_Profit),
                          y = Total_Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4.2, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels  = dollar_format(),
                     expand  = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.28))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Total Profit by Category", x = NULL, y = "Total Profit") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

# Panel C: Margin vs % Profitable bubble
p_cat_bubble <- ggplot(category_metrics,
                       aes(x = Profit_Margin, y = Pct_Profitable,
                           color = Category, size = Total_Sales,
                           label = Category)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 4, fontface = "bold", show.legend = FALSE,
                  box.padding = 0.5) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_size_continuous(labels = dollar_format(), name = "Total Sales",
                        range = c(8, 18)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%"),
                     limits = c(50, 100)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Margin vs % Profitable Transactions",
    subtitle = "Bubble size ∝ Total Sales",
    x = "Overall Profit Margin (%)",
    y = "% of Transactions with Profit > 0"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"), legend.position = "none")

(p_cat_box | p_cat_total) / p_cat_bubble















Tip



Findings:

Technology leads with $146,543 in total profit and a 17.4% profit margin, despite having fewer transactions than Office Supplies.
The bubble chart reveals that higher margin does not always mean higher total profit — transaction volume matters.
Furniture shows the lowest % of profitable transactions, indicating a systemic pricing or discounting problem in that category.

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
4.2 By Sub-Category

Lollipop ChartTreemap




Show Code
subcategory_metrics <- superstore %>%
  group_by(`Sub-Category`, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    Avg_Profit   = mean(Profit),
    Count        = n(),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(Total_Profit)

# Colour flag
subcategory_metrics <- subcategory_metrics %>%
  mutate(Profitable = Total_Profit > 0)

ggplot(subcategory_metrics,
       aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, Total_Profit),
           y = Total_Profit, color = Profitable)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = reorder(`Sub-Category`, Total_Profit), yend = 0),
               linewidth = 1.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(size = 5.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1),
        hjust = ifelse(Total_Profit >= 0, -0.18, 1.18)),
    size = 3.0, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#27ae60", "FALSE" = "#e74c3c"),
                     labels  = c("FALSE" = "Loss-making", "TRUE" = "Profitable"),
                     name    = "Status") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.2, 0.2))) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_grid(Category ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Total Profit by Sub-Category",
    subtitle = "Grouped by main Category  |  Red = loss-making sub-categories",
    x = NULL, y = "Total Profit",
    caption  = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    strip.text      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11,
                                   color = "white",
                                   margin = margin(4, 4, 4, 4)),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#2c3e50", color = NA),
    legend.position  = "bottom"
  )













Show Code
subcategory_tm <- subcategory_metrics %>%
  mutate(
    abs_profit = abs(Total_Profit),
    tile_label = paste0(`Sub-Category`, "\n",
                        dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1))
  )

ggplot(subcategory_tm,
       aes(area      = abs_profit,
           fill      = Total_Profit,
           label     = tile_label,
           subgroup  = Category)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(color = "white", linewidth = 3) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_text(
    place = "topleft", grow = FALSE, reflow = TRUE,
    colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 14, alpha = 0.65
  ) +
  geom_treemap_text(
    color = "white", place = "centre",
    reflow = TRUE, size = 9, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "#e74c3c",
    mid      = "#f5f5f5",
    high     = "#27ae60",
    midpoint = 0,
    labels   = dollar_format(),
    name     = "Total Profit"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Treemap: Sub-Category Profitability",
    subtitle = "Tile size ∝ |Profit|   |   Colour: Green = Profitable · Red = Loss",
    caption  = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15),
    legend.position = "right"
  )


















Warning



Critical Finding:
3 sub-categories are generating net losses: Tables (-$17,753.21), Bookcases (-$3,632.07), Supplies (-$1,171.39). The worst is Tables at –$17,753.21. These require immediate strategic review — consider repricing, capping discounts, or discontinuation.

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
7.1 Sales & Profit Trends


Show Code
monthly_by_cat <- superstore %>%
  group_by(YearMonth, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

monthly_overall <- superstore %>%
  group_by(YearMonth) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales   = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit  = sum(Profit),
    Profit_Margin = Total_Profit / Total_Sales * 100,
    .groups       = "drop"
  )

# Sales trend by category
p_sales_trend <- ggplot(monthly_by_cat,
                        aes(x = YearMonth, y = Total_Sales, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category",
       x = NULL, y = "Total Sales", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Profit trend by category
p_profit_trend <- ggplot(monthly_by_cat,
                         aes(x = YearMonth, y = Total_Profit, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(title = "Monthly Profit Trend by Category",
       x = NULL, y = "Total Profit", color = "Category") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# Profit margin area + LOESS
p_margin_trend <- ggplot(monthly_overall,
                          aes(x = YearMonth, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#3498db", alpha = 0.18) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c",
              fill = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), "%")) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Overall Profit Margin",
    subtitle = "Red LOESS line = long-run trend  |  Shaded band = 95% CI",
    x = NULL, y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

p_sales_trend / p_profit_trend / p_margin_trend

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
7.2 Seasonal Patterns


Show Code
heatmap_data <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Year, Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Total_Sales  = sum(Sales),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

# Month × Year profit heatmap
p_heatmap <- ggplot(heatmap_data,
                    aes(x = Month, y = factor(Year), fill = Total_Profit)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 0.1, scale = 0.001, suffix = "K")),
    size = 3.2, fontface = "bold", color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#e74c3c", mid = "grey88", high = "#27ae60",
    midpoint = 0, name = "Total Profit",
    labels = dollar_format()
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Profit Heatmap — Year × Month",
    subtitle = "Green = high profit  |  Red = low or negative profit",
    x = "Month", y = "Year"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )

# Avg monthly sales ridge across years
monthly_avg <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Month, Category) %>%
  summarise(Avg_Sales = mean(Sales), .groups = "drop")

p_season <- ggplot(monthly_avg,
                   aes(x = Month, y = Avg_Sales, fill = Category)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.85, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title    = "Average Monthly Sales by Category",
    subtitle = "Seasonal peaks are most visible in Q4 (Oct–Dec)",
    x = "Month", y = "Avg Sales per Month",
    fill = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

p_season / p_heatmap















Tip



Temporal Findings:

Dec is consistently the strongest month, averaging $11,057.46 in profit — likely driven by end-of-year purchasing.
Jan is the weakest, averaging $2,374.31.
The LOESS trend in the margin chart reveals the long-run health trajectory of the business.

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
7.3 Interactive Time Series (Plotly)


Show Code
p_anim <- plot_ly(
  monthly_by_cat,
  x      = ~YearMonth,
  y      = ~Total_Profit,

) %>%

    title = list(
      text = "<b>Monthly Profit by Category (Interactive)</b><br><sup>Drag slider to zoom — hover for details</sup>"
    ),
    xaxis = list(
      title       = "",

    ),

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
9.1 Performance Summary


Show Code
# Compute all metrics dynamically — no hardcoding
# Compute all metrics dynamically — no hardcoding
pct_loss_txns <- mean(superstore$Profit < 0)

summary_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales Revenue",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Total Loss (Unprofitable Orders)",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category",
    "Best Region (Total Profit)",
    "Best Region (Profit Margin)",
    "Avg Discount — Profitable Orders",
    "Avg Discount — Unprofitable Orders"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round(sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales) * 100, 2), " %"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","),
           "  (", round(pct_loss_txns * 100, 1), " % of orders)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    superstore %>% group_by(Category) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_max(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(Category),
    superstore %>% group_by(`Sub-Category`) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_min(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(`Sub-Category`),
    superstore %>% group_by(Region) %>%
      summarise(tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      slice_max(tp, n = 1) %>% pull(Region),
    superstore %>% group_by(Region) %>%
      summarise(ts = sum(Sales), tp = sum(Profit), .groups = "drop") %>%
      mutate(margin = tp / ts) %>%
      slice_max(margin, n = 1) %>% pull(Region),
    percent(mean(superstore$Discount[superstore$Profit > 0]), accuracy = 0.1),
    percent(mean(superstore$Discount[superstore$Profit <= 0]), accuracy = 0.1)
  )
)

title: In-class Exercise 2
text:
9.2 Interactive Profitability Dashboard


Show Code
fig_cat <- plot_ly(
  category_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Category, Total_Profit),
  y    = ~Total_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Category,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Set2"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Category, "</b><br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "Profit Margin: ", round(Profit_Margin, 1), "%<br>",
    "Transactions: ", format(Count, big.mark = ",")
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Category</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Total Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.0f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

fig_reg <- plot_ly(
  regional_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Region, Total_Profit),
  y    = ~Total_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Region,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(4, "Set3"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Region, "</b><br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "Profit Margin: ", round(Profit_Margin, 1), "%<br>",
    "Transactions: ", format(Count, big.mark = ",")
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Region</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Total Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.0f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

fig_seg <- plot_ly(
  segment_metrics,
  x    = ~reorder(Segment, Avg_Profit),
  y    = ~Avg_Profit,
  type = "bar",
  color = ~Segment,
  colors = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(3, "Dark2"),
  text = ~paste0(
    "<b>", Segment, "</b><br>",
    "Avg Profit: ", dollar(Avg_Profit, accuracy = 0.01), "<br>",
    "Total Profit: ", dollar(Total_Profit), "<br>",
    "% Profitable: ", round(Pct_Profitable, 1), "%"
  ),
  hovertemplate = "%{text}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title   = "<b>Customer Segment</b>",
    xaxis   = list(title = ""),
    yaxis   = list(title = "Avg Profit ($)", tickformat = "$,.2f"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )

subplot(fig_cat, fig_reg, fig_seg, nrows = 1,
        titleX = TRUE, titleY = TRUE, shareY = FALSE,
        margin = 0.06) %>%
  layout(
    title = list(
      text = "<b>Superstore Profitability Dashboard</b><br><sup>Hover over bars for details</sup>",
      x    = 0.5
    ),
    plot_bgcolor  = "rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    paper_bgcolor = "rgba(0,0,0,0)"
  )












NoteBusiness Recommendations



1. Discount Strategy Reform

Implement a hard discount cap at 30 % — data shows that losses spike sharply beyond this threshold, especially in Furniture.
Profitable orders average 8.0% discount; unprofitable average 46.9% — closing this gap is the single highest-leverage action available.
Require management sign-off for any discount exceeding 20 %.

2. Product Line Optimisation

Loss-making sub-categories requiring action: Tables (-$17,753.21) · Bookcases (-$3,632.07) · Supplies (-$1,171.39).
Consider repricing or discontinuing Tables (worst performer).
Double down on: Copiers, Phones, Accessories — the top-3 most profitable sub-categories.

3. Regional Strategy

West region: proven performance — maintain investment and use as the benchmark.
Central region: diagnose root causes — is it discount abuse, product mix, or market conditions? Implement corrective actions in the next quarter.

4. Segment Development

Home Office: highest profit per transaction — grow this segment through targeted outreach and premium product bundles.
Consumer: high volume, lower margin — focus campaigns on reducing discount frequency rather than acquiring more customers.
Corporate: offer value-added services, SLAs, and dedicated account management instead of price discounts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Welcome! This hands-on exercise covers two complementary tools for animated visualisation in R.
When telling a visually-driven data story, animated graphics tends to attract the interest of the audience and make deeper impressions than static graphics. In this hands-on exercise, we learn how to create animated data visualisations using gganimate and plotly R packages. We also learn how to:

Reshape data using the tidyr package
Process, wrangle and transform data using the dplyr package

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
2.1 Terminology
Before diving into creating animated statistical graphs, it is important to understand the key concepts:

Frame: Each frame represents a different point in time or a different category. When the frame changes, the data points on the graph are updated to reflect the new data.
Animation Attributes: Settings that control how the animation behaves — such as the duration of each frame, the easing function used to transition between frames, and whether to start from the current frame or from the beginning.







TipWhen Should You Animate?



Before making animated graphs, ask: Does it make sense to go through the effort?

For exploratory data analysis: an animated graphic may not be worth the time investment.
For presentations: a few well-placed animated graphics can help an audience connect with your topic remarkably better than static counterparts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
3.1 Loading the R Packages
The following R packages are used in this exercise:







Package
Purpose




readxl
Import Excel workbooks


gifski
Convert video frames to GIF animations with efficient cross-frame palettes


gapminder
Provides the country_colors colour scheme


plotly
Interactive statistical graphs with animation support


gganimate
Extend ggplot2 grammar to include animation


tidyverse
Data wrangling, transformation and static visualisation




pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse,
               ggplot2, dplyr, patchwork, scales)







NoteWhat I Learned



Using pacman::p_load() is a cleaner alternative to repeated library() calls — it installs missing packages automatically and loads all listed packages in one line. This keeps the setup code concise and reproducible across different machines.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
3.2 Importing the Data
The Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation Excel workbook is used throughout this exercise. The data captures population statistics (% aged, % young, total population) across countries and continents over multiple years.
Evolution of the import code — from deprecated to modern dplyr:

Original (Deprecated)Intermediate (mutate_at)Modern (across — recommended)



# mutate_each_() deprecated in dplyr 0.7.0; funs() deprecated in 0.8.0
col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate_each_(funs(factor(.)), col) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))




# mutate_at() works but is superseded — prefer across()
col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate_at(col, as.factor) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))




col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))










NoteWhat I Learned




read_xls() from readxl handles Excel files natively without saving as CSV first.
The shift from mutate_each_() + funs() → mutate_at() → mutate(across()) reflects how the dplyr API matured toward a more consistent, expressive design. across() is the modern standard and works seamlessly inside any mutate() or summarise() call.
Converting Year to integer (not double) is critical for transition_time() to treat it correctly as a discrete time variable.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
section: 5.1 Building a Static Population Bubble Plot
text:
5.1 Building a Static Population Bubble Plot
Before animating, we first build the static version. This helps verify the visual design and aesthetics before adding motion.


Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                      size = Population,
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title    = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Static version — all years overlaid",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 22, face = "bold", colour = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, colour = "#7f8c8d")
  )





Static bubble plot — all years overlaid (note the {frame_time} placeholder in title)










NoteWhat I Learned



The static plot with all years overlaid creates visual noise — overlapping bubbles make it impossible to distinguish temporal change. The {frame_time} placeholder in the title does not yet render because there is no transition_time() applied. This clearly shows why animation adds value: without it, the temporal narrative is completely hidden.
scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) is critical — it maps raw population values (which span orders of magnitude) to a visually sensible dot size range so small countries remain visible.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
5.2 Building the Animated Bubble Plot
Now we add the gganimate layer. transition_time(Year) slices the data by Year and renders one frame per year, creating the impression of temporal movement.

Linear (default)With Shadow Wake (trajectories)




Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                      size = Population,
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title    = "Year: {frame_time}",
       subtitle = "Each bubble = one country  |  Size = Population",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 26, face = "bold", colour = "#c0392b"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9,  colour = "#555555")
  )





Animated bubble chart — countries shift rightward (ageing) over time








Show code
ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                      size = Population,
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title    = "Year: {frame_time}  |  Trails show historical path",
       subtitle = "Longer trail = faster demographic shift",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear") +
  shadow_wake(wake_length = 0.2, alpha = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 24, face = "bold", colour = "#c0392b"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9,  colour = "#555555")
  )





shadow_wake() reveals each country’s historical trajectory as a fading trail













NoteWhat I Learned




transition_time(Year) is the core animation function. It automatically creates one frame per unique Year value and interpolates bubble positions between frames using the easing function.
ease_aes("linear") makes each aesthetic change at a constant rate. Alternative easing methods (quadratic, elastic, bounce, etc.) create different “feel” — linear is most honest for demographic data where we want precise temporal mapping.
shadow_wake() leaves a fading “wake” behind each bubble, making individual country trajectories visible during the animation. The wake_length parameter controls how much history is shown (0 = none, 1 = full history). Countries ageing rapidly show long rightward trails versus more stable countries — analytical insight that would otherwise require a separate panel.
The animated GIF makes the rightward drift (increasing % Aged) and downward drift (decreasing % Young) immediately obvious — the global demographic transition becomes a visual narrative rather than a table of numbers.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
section: 6.1 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: ggplotly() Method
text:
6.1 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: ggplotly() Method

First Attempt — Legend IssueFixed — theme(legend.position = 'none')


ggplotly() converts a ggplot2 object into an interactive plotly widget. Note the known issue with show.legend = FALSE:


Show code
gg <- ggplot(globalPop,
             aes(x = Old, y = Young,
                 size = Population,
                 colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size  = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha       = 0.7,
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young")

ggplotly(gg)




ggplotly animation — legend still appears despite show.legend = FALSE








NoteWhat I Learned



show.legend = FALSE inside geom_point() does not suppress the legend in ggplotly(). This is because ggplotly() translates each colour mapping to a separate plotly trace, and trace visibility is controlled separately from ggplot2’s legend logic. The fix is to use theme(legend.position = 'none') at the plot level — which plotly does respect during conversion.






Show code
gg <- ggplot(globalPop,
             aes(x      = Old,
                 y      = Young,
                 size   = Population,
                 colour = Country,
                 frame  = Year)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplotly(gg)




ggplotly animation — legend correctly suppressed using theme()








NoteWhat I Learned



The animated bubble plot rendered by ggplotly() includes a play/pause button and a year slider — two interactive controls that the gganimate GIF cannot provide. This interactivity fundamentally changes the viewer experience:

The play/pause allows a presenter to stop at a specific year to discuss it in detail.
The slider allows viewers to manually scrub through time — enabling personalised exploration.
Hover tooltips show exact values for each country’s bubble on demand.

This is the key trade-off: gganimate produces a self-running GIF ideal for reports or social media, while plotly produces an interactive widget ideal for web dashboards and exploratory analysis.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
section: 6.2 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: plot_ly() Method
text:
6.2 Building an Animated Bubble Plot: plot_ly() Method
plot_ly() provides more direct control over plotly’s native API. Here we colour by Continent rather than Country, making continental patterns easier to distinguish.

Basic plot_ly()With ids — Object ConstancyEnhanced — Rich Hover Tooltips




Show code
bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x         = ~Old,
          y         = ~Young,
          size      = ~Population,
          color     = ~Continent,
          sizes     = c(2, 60),
          frame     = ~Year,
          text      = ~Country,
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type      = "scatter",
          mode      = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE,
         xaxis = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis = list(title = "% Young"),
         title = list(text  = "Global Demographics Over Time"))
bp




plot_ly() animated bubble chart — coloured by Continent, hover shows country name








NoteWhat I Learned



plot_ly() gives finer control than ggplotly():

sizes = c(2, 60) maps the population range to plotly’s native size scale (different units from ggplot2’s scale_size()).
hoverinfo = "text" + text = ~Country gives clean, custom tooltips showing only the country name.
color = ~Continent (continent-level colouring) is analytically superior to country-level colouring for spotting continental clusters — Africa’s young/low-aged cluster vs Europe’s aged/low-young cluster become immediately clear.
frame = ~Year is the native plotly equivalent of transition_time(Year) in gganimate — but plotly renders this as a JavaScript-driven interactive animation, not a pre-rendered GIF.





Adding ids = ~Country ensures each bubble maintains identity across frames, enabling smooth interpolation:


Show code
bp_ids <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x         = ~Old,
          y         = ~Young,
          size      = ~Population,
          color     = ~Continent,
          sizes     = c(2, 60),
          frame     = ~Year,
          ids       = ~Country,
          text      = ~Country,
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type      = "scatter",
          mode      = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis  = list(title = "% Aged"),
         yaxis  = list(title = "% Young"),
         title  = list(text  = "Global Demographics — Smooth Transitions (ids = Country)"),
         legend = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_ids




plot_ly() with ids for smooth object constancy across animation frames








NoteWhat I Learned



Object constancy is a fundamental principle of animated visualisation design: viewers should be able to follow a specific object as it moves across frames. Without ids, plotly may re-match bubbles arbitrarily between frames, causing confusing “teleportation” of bubbles. Adding ids = ~Country tells plotly which bubble in frame N corresponds to which bubble in frame N+1, enabling smooth, continuous motion. This makes individual country trajectories trackable by eye — a critical advantage for analytical storytelling.




A presentation-ready version with multi-line custom tooltips for the most important countries:


Show code
bp_enhanced <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x         = ~Old,
          y         = ~Young,
          size      = ~Population,
          color     = ~Continent,
          sizes     = c(2, 100),
          frame     = ~Year,
          ids       = ~Country,
          text      = ~paste0("<b>", Country, "</b><br>",
                              "Population: ", scales::comma(Population), "<br>",
                              "% Aged: ",     round(Old,   1), "%<br>",
                              "% Young: ",    round(Young, 1), "%"),
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type      = "scatter",
          mode      = "markers") %>%
  layout(showlegend = TRUE,
         xaxis  = list(title = "% Aged Population"),
         yaxis  = list(title = "% Young Population"),
         title  = list(text  = "Global Demographic Transition Over Time"),
         legend = list(title = list(text = "Continent")))
bp_enhanced




Enhanced plot_ly() — rich tooltips with full country statistics on hover








NoteWhat I Learned



Combining ids with rich hover text (paste0() for multi-line tooltips) creates a presentation-quality chart. The custom tooltip reveals Population, % Aged, and % Young simultaneously on hover — providing detailed information without cluttering the visual. This illustrates the key analytical advantage of interactive plotly animations over static charts: viewers can dive deeper into specific data points without the analyst needing to pre-decide what to highlight.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
Welcome! This hands-on exercise covers three complementary tools for interactive visualisation in R:

ggiraph — extends ggplot2 with hover tooltips, data_id linking, and onclick actions
plotly — full-featured interactive charts via plot_ly() and ggplotly()
crosstalk — cross-widget interactions linking plots to interactive tables

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.1 Learning Outcome
In this hands-on exercise, you will learn how to create interactive data visualisations by using functions provided by the ggiraph and plotly packages. Specifically, we will explore:

How to add hover tooltips to ggplot2 charts using geom_*_interactive()
How to link multiple charts with shared data_id keys for coordinated highlighting
How to deploy onclick actions that open external URLs from chart elements
How to convert ggplot2 objects to interactive plotly widgets with ggplotly()
How to build linked brushing dashboards using the crosstalk SharedData pattern

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.2 Getting Started
First, check, install and launch the following R packages:







Package
Purpose




ggiraph
Makes ggplot2 graphics interactive via tooltip, data_id and onclick


plotly
Interactive web graphics via plot_ly() and ggplotly()


DT
R interface to JavaScript DataTables for interactive HTML tables


tidyverse
Modern R packages for data science (ggplot2, dplyr, readr, etc.)


patchwork
Combines multiple ggplot2 graphs; enables coordinated ggiraph views


readxl
Imports Excel workbooks


viridis
Perceptually uniform colour palettes


scales
Formatting helpers for axis labels and tooltips


crosstalk
Cross-widget linked brushing and filtering between htmlwidgets





Show code
pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, DT, tidyverse, readxl,
               patchwork, viridis, scales, crosstalk)








NoteWhat I Learned



pacman::p_load() installs any missing package before loading — one line replaces multiple install.packages() + library() calls. This keeps setup code portable across machines without manual dependency management.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.3 Importing Data
The Data worksheet from the GlobalPopulation Excel workbook is used throughout this exercise. It contains country-level demographic records including: Country, Continent, Year, Population, Young (% young-age population) and Old (% elderly population).


Show code
pop_data <- read_xls("data/GlobalPopulation.xls", sheet = "Data") %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor))








NoteWhat I Learned



read_xls() from readxl handles Excel files natively — no CSV conversion needed. mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor)) is the modern dplyr idiom for bulk type conversion: where(is.character) acts as a column selector predicate, so any new character column added later is also automatically converted. This is more robust than listing column names manually.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.4 Visual Data Exploration
Before building interactive charts, it is essential to understand the structure and distribution of the data. Visual exploration helps identify patterns, skewness, and grouping variables that will guide interactive design choices.

Dataset OverviewCategorical DistributionsNumeric DistributionsVariable Relationships




Show code
n_records  <- nrow(pop_data)
n_complete <- sum(complete.cases(pop_data))

var_types <- data.frame(Type = sapply(pop_data, function(x) class(x)[1])) %>%
  count(Type)

p_sample <- ggplot(data.frame(x = 1, y = 1), aes(x, y)) +
  geom_tile(fill = "#bfdbfe", alpha = 0.6, width = 0.9, height = 0.9) +
  geom_text(label = paste0("n = ", format(n_records, big.mark = ","), "\nrecords"),
            size = 9, fontface = "bold", colour = "#1e3a5f") +
  labs(title = "Sample Size") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14))

p_types <- ggplot(var_types, aes(x = reorder(Type, -n), y = n, fill = Type)) +
  geom_col(alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("factor"  = "#a5b4fc",
                               "integer" = "#bfdbfe",
                               "numeric" = "#93c5fd")) +
  labs(title = "Variable Types", x = "Type", y = "Count") +
  ylim(0, max(var_types$n) * 1.25) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
        legend.position = "none")

missing_df <- reshape2::melt(is.na(pop_data))

p_missing <- ggplot(missing_df, aes(x = Var2, y = Var1, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#10b981", "TRUE" = "#ef4444"),
                    labels = c("Complete", "Missing"), name = "Status") +
  labs(title = "Missing Value Heatmap", x = "Variables", y = "Observations") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
        axis.text.y   = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y  = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x   = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        panel.grid    = element_blank())

(p_sample | p_types) / p_missing +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GlobalPopulation Dataset Overview",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))
  ) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1.4))















NoteWhat I Learned



The overview reveals that the dataset is 100% complete — an important confirmation before building interactive charts, since missing values can cause silent tooltip failures or blank hover panels. The variable type breakdown confirms that Country and Continent are factors (suitable for data_id grouping) while Young, Old, and Population are numeric (suitable for axis mappings and tooltip statistics).






Show code
cat_vars      <- names(pop_data)[sapply(pop_data, is.factor)]
pastel_colors <- c("#bfdbfe","#a5b4fc","#bbf7d0","#fde68a",
                   "#fecaca","#fbcfe8","#a7f3d0","#ddd6fe")

plot_list <- lapply(cat_vars, function(var) {
  plot_data <- pop_data %>%
    group_by(.data[[var]]) %>%
    summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
    mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
    arrange(desc(n))

  n_cats    <- nrow(plot_data)
  fills     <- rep_len(pastel_colors, n_cats)
  title_sfx <- ""
  if (n_cats > 20) { plot_data <- slice_head(plot_data, n = 20); title_sfx <- " (Top 20)" }

  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = reorder(.data[[var]], -n), y = n, fill = .data[[var]])) +
    geom_col(alpha = 0.9, width = 0.7, colour = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = paste0(n, "\n(", round(pct, 1), "%)")),
              vjust = -0.2, size = 2.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "#1e3a5f") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fills) +
    labs(title = paste(var, "Distribution", title_sfx), y = "Count", x = "") +
    ylim(0, max(plot_data$n) * 1.18) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(plot.title      = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 11),
          legend.position = "none",
          axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))
})

wrap_plots(plot_list, ncol = 2) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Categorical Variable Distributions",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))
  )















NoteWhat I Learned



Africa has the most country-records in the dataset while Oceania has the fewest. This imbalance matters for interactive design: continent-level data_id grouping will produce dense hover clusters for Africa and sparse ones for Oceania. Understanding this distribution before coding helps anticipate which interactions will be visually clear versus confusing.






Show code
latest_year <- max(pop_data$Year, na.rm = TRUE)
latest_snap <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == latest_year)

ph1 <- ggplot(latest_snap, aes(x = Old)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#3b82f6", colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = paste("% Aged —", latest_year), x = "% Aged", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

ph2 <- ggplot(latest_snap, aes(x = Young)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#ec4899", colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = paste("% Young —", latest_year), x = "% Young", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

ph3 <- ggplot(latest_snap, aes(x = Population / 1e6)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#10b981", colour = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
  labs(title = paste("Population (M) —", latest_year),
       x = "Population (Millions)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

pb1 <- ggplot(latest_snap, aes(x = Continent, y = Old, fill = Continent)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.size = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "% Aged by Continent", x = "", y = "% Aged") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

(ph1 | ph2 | ph3) / pb1 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Numeric Variable Distributions",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))
  ) +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1.3))















NoteWhat I Learned



Population is highly right-skewed — a handful of very populous countries dominate the scale. This explains why interactive charts use sizes = c(2, 100) in plotly or scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) in ggplot2 to compress the mapping and keep small countries visible. The boxplot confirms that Europe has the highest median ageing percentage — a finding that interactive hover tooltips will let users drill into at the country level.






Show code
r1 <- ggplot(latest_snap, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2.5) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young (latest year)",
       subtitle = "Strong negative correlation — the demographic trade-off",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal()

r2 <- pop_data %>%
  group_by(Continent, Year) %>%
  summarise(Mean_Old = mean(Old, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Mean_Old, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Mean % Aged Over Time by Continent",
       subtitle = "All continents ageing — rate differs",
       x = "Year", y = "Mean % Aged") +
  theme_minimal()

r1 / r2 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Key Data Relationships",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5))
  )















NoteWhat I Learned



The strong negative correlation between % Aged and % Young is the central analytical story of this dataset. Understanding it upfront directly motivates the interactive design choices ahead: data_id = Continent for group highlighting (to compare continental trajectories), and tooltips showing both Old and Young values simultaneously (to reveal the demographic trade-off at country level in a single hover).

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.5 Interactive Data Visualisation — ggiraph Methods






TipCore Concept



ggiraph is an htmlwidget and ggplot2 extension. It makes ggplot graphics interactive via three aesthetics added to geom_*_interactive() functions:

tooltip — text shown when mouse hovers over an element
data_id — key that links elements for group highlighting
onclick — JavaScript string executed on click

Workflow: replace geom_*() → geom_*_interactive(), add interactive aesthetics inside aes(), then wrap the ggplot object in girafe().



1.5.1 Tooltip Effect with tooltip Aesthetic
Below is a typical code pattern for an interactive statistical graph using ggiraph. Two steps are involved: first build a ggplot object with an interactive geom; then pass it to girafe() to generate an SVG widget displayed on the HTML page.


Show code
p <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Old, y = Young)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = Country),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#1e40af"
  ) +
  labs(title  = "% Aged vs % Young — Basic Tooltip (hover for country name)",
       subtitle = paste("Latest year:", max(pop_data$Year, na.rm = TRUE)),
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






By hovering the mouse pointer on any data point, the country name is displayed. The only change from a standard ggplot is replacing geom_point() with geom_point_interactive() and adding tooltip = Country inside aes(), then wrapping in girafe().






NoteWhat I Learned



The two-step pattern — interactive geom → girafe() — is the mental model for all ggiraph work. The SVG output embeds directly in HTML reports with no extra server required. This is the cleanest entry point: one swapped geom + one wrapper, and you have immediate hover feedback. Critically, the tooltip must reference a column that already exists in the data frame — you cannot compute values inline inside aes() the way you can with colour = or size =.





1.5.2 Displaying Multiple Information on Tooltip
Tooltip content can be customised by constructing a character column that combines multiple fields:


Show code
latest_multi <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country = ",    Country,
    "\nContinent = ", Continent,
    "\n% Aged = ",   round(Old, 1),   "%",
    "\n% Young = ",  round(Young, 1), "%"
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_multi, aes(x = Old, y = Young)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#059669"
  ) +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young — Multi-field Tooltip",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






The first mutate() call creates a new tooltip_text column. At the same time, it populates text from Country, Continent, Old, and Young fields into the newly created field, which is then passed to aes(tooltip = tooltip_text).
By hovering on a data point, Country, Continent, % Aged, and % Young are all displayed together.






NoteWhat I Learned



Pre-computing a tooltip column with mutate() before the ggplot call keeps aes() clean and makes complex tooltips easy to debug — you can inspect the tooltip column directly with head(). Using explicit field labels (e.g., "Country = ") makes tooltips self-explanatory for end users who may not recognise which axis maps to which variable.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.6 Interactivity — Customisation

1.6.1 Customising Tooltip Style
opts_tooltip() inside girafe() applies CSS to the tooltip container, allowing full visual control over its appearance:


Show code
tooltip_css <- "background-color:#1e293b; color:#f8fafc; font-weight:bold;
                padding:8px 12px; border-radius:6px; font-size:13px;"

latest_styled <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country = ",    Country,
    "\nContinent = ", Continent
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_styled, aes(x = Old, y = Young)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#7c3aed"
  ) +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young — Styled Tooltip (dark background)",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_tooltip(css = tooltip_css)
  )
)






The tooltip_css string is a standard CSS declaration. opts_tooltip(css = ...) passes it to the SVG tooltip element. The same CSS properties available in web development — background-color, color, font-weight, border-radius — work here. Refer to Customizing girafe objects for a full list of options.






NoteWhat I Learned



Tooltip CSS is applied at the girafe() level, not inside aes() — this cleanly separates data mapping from visual styling. Dark backgrounds with light text (#1e293b / #f8fafc) immediately distinguish the tooltip from the chart background, improving readability regardless of the chart’s colour scheme. This mirrors best practices from web UI design applied directly to R visualisations.





1.6.2 Displaying Statistics on Tooltip
A function can compute derived statistics and embed them in the tooltip using after_stat():


Show code
tooltip_fn <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = 0.01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem_val  <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean % Aged:", mean_val, "+/-", sem_val)
}

latest_stat <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

gg_bar <- ggplot(data = latest_stat, aes(x = Continent)) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y       = Old,
        tooltip = after_stat(tooltip_fn(y, ymax))),
    fun.data = "mean_se",
    geom     = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill     = "#93c5fd"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(y = Old),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom     = "errorbar",
    width    = 0.2, linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  labs(title    = "Mean % Aged by Continent — Statistics on Tooltip",
       subtitle  = "Hover each bar to see Mean ± SEM",
       x = "Continent", y = "Mean % Aged") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

girafe(ggobj = gg_bar, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10 * 0.618)






after_stat() defers evaluation of tooltip_fn(y, ymax) until stat_summary has computed the summary statistics — meaning y and ymax refer to the computed mean and upper bound (mean + SE), not raw data columns. GeomInteractiveCol is the interactive version of GeomCol, enabling the tooltip on bar charts.






NoteWhat I Learned



after_stat() is the bridge between statistical transformations and interactive aesthetics. Without it, computed values like y (mean) and ymax (mean + SE) would not be accessible inside aes(). This pattern is essential whenever you want to show computed statistics — not raw values — on hover. For demographic data, showing Mean ± SEM on hover turns a simple bar chart into a rigorous statistical summary without cluttering the visual with permanent annotations.





1.6.3 Hover Effect with data_id Aesthetic
data_id is the second interactive feature of ggiraph. Elements sharing the same data_id value are highlighted together on hover:


Show code
latest_id <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_id, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Hover to highlight all countries in the same continent",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618
)






Elements associated with a data_id (i.e. Continent) will be highlighted upon mouse over. The default value of the hover CSS is hover_css = "fill:orange;". Note that data_id and tooltip are independent: you can use either or both.






NoteWhat I Learned



data_id is the mechanism that makes ggiraph’s linking magic possible. Assigning the same key to multiple elements (all points from Asia, for example) causes them to respond as a group. This transforms a simple hover into a powerful grouping tool — the viewer can immediately see all members of a continent cluster without needing colour legend lookup or filtering. This is the foundation for coordinated multiple views later.





1.6.4 Styling the Hover Effect
CSS codes inside opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv() change the highlighting colour and dim non-hovered elements:


Show code
latest_hover <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_hover, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Styled hover: hovered continent bold, others dimmed",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.15;")
  )
)






Different from the previous example, here the CSS customisation requests are encoded directly inside opts_hover() and opts_hover_inv(). opts_hover() defines the CSS for the hovered element; opts_hover_inv() applies to all other elements.






NoteWhat I Learned



The opts_hover_inv() pattern — dim everything else — is far more effective than highlighting the active element alone. The contrast between opacity:0.15 (background countries) and the bold dark fill (hovered continent) creates immediate visual hierarchy with only two CSS lines. Applied to demographic data, this lets the viewer instantly isolate one continent’s position in the % Aged vs % Young space without losing context of the full distribution.





1.6.5 Combining Tooltip and Hover Effect
There are times we want to combine tooltip and hover effect on the interactive statistical graph:


Show code
latest_combo <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "Country: ",     Country,
    "\nContinent: ", Continent,
    "\n% Aged: ",    round(Old, 1),   "%",
    "\n% Young: ",   round(Young, 1), "%",
    "\nPopulation: ", format(Population, big.mark = ",")
  ))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_combo, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text,
        data_id = Continent),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
  labs(title = "Combined: tooltip shows country detail, hover highlights continent",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.15;")
  )
)






Elements associated with a data_id (i.e. Continent) will be highlighted upon mouse over. At the same time, the tooltip will show the individual country’s full demographic profile.






NoteWhat I Learned



Combining tooltip and data_id delivers two analytical layers at once — individual context (which country, what values) via tooltip, and group context (which continent, which cluster) via hover highlighting. For hierarchical datasets like country-within-continent, this layered interactivity matches the natural analytical workflow: first notice a cluster, then explore specific countries within it. This is impossible to achieve with static charts.





1.6.6 Click Effect with onclick
onclick argument of ggiraph provides hotlink interactivity on the web. The code chunk below shows an example:


Show code
latest_click <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(
    onclick_js   = paste0(
      "window.open('https://www.google.com/search?q=",
      URLencode(paste(as.character(Country), "population demographics")),
      "')"
    ),
    tooltip_text = paste0(as.character(Country),
                          "\n(click to open Google search)")
  )

p <- ggplot(data = latest_click, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = tooltip_text,
        onclick  = onclick_js),
    size = 3.5, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Click any country point to open a Google search for that country",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))

girafe(
  ggobj      = p,
  width_svg  = 10,
  height_svg = 10 * 0.618,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css = "fill:#FF3333; cursor:pointer;")
  )
)






Web document link with a data object will be displayed on the web browser upon mouse click.






Warning



Note that click actions must be a string column in the dataset containing valid JavaScript instructions. The onclick value is evaluated as raw JavaScript in the browser — errors in the JS string will silently fail.








NoteWhat I Learned



The onclick aesthetic turns every data point into a hyperlink — bridging the visualisation to any external information system. cursor:pointer in opts_hover() signals clickability to users without additional UI elements. Always pre-compute the JS string as a data column (never build it inside aes()), and always use URLencode() for country names to handle spaces and special characters correctly.





1.6.7 Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
When a data point of one of the scatter plots is selected, the corresponding data point on the second visualisation will be highlighted too.
In order to build coordinated multiple views, the following programming strategy is used:

Appropriate interactive functions of ggiraph are used to create the multiple views.
patchwork function of the patchwork package is used inside girafe() via the code argument to create the interactive coordinated multiple views.



Show code
latest_coord <- pop_data %>%
  filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(tooltip_text = paste0(
    "<b>", Country, "</b><br>",
    "Continent: ", Continent, "<br>",
    "% Aged: ",   round(Old, 1),   "%<br>",
    "% Young: ",  round(Young, 1), "%"
  ))

p1 <- ggplot(data = latest_coord, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Country,
        tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

p2 <- ggplot(data = latest_coord, aes(x = Old, y = Population / 1e6, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point_interactive(
    aes(data_id = Country,
        tooltip = tooltip_text),
    size = 3, alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "turbo") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs Population (Millions)",
       x = "% Aged", y = "Population (M)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))

girafe(
  code       = print(p1 + p2),
  width_svg  = 12,
  height_svg = 4.5,
  options    = list(
    opts_hover(css     = "fill:#202020; stroke:#202020;"),
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
  )
)






The data_id aesthetic is critical to link observations between plots — here Country is the shared key. The tooltip aesthetic is optional but provides useful per-point detail when hovering.






ImportantWhat I Learned



Coordinated views require two conditions: (1) identical data_id keys across all panels — Country here — and (2) the patchwork combination placed inside girafe() via the code argument rather than ggobj. When both conditions are met, ggiraph handles all JavaScript linking automatically. This enables a powerful workflow: identify an outlier in the demographic scatter (left panel) and instantly see its population size context (right panel) — something a static chart grid can never achieve.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.7 Interactive Data Visualisation — plotly Methods






TipCore Concept



plotly’s R library creates interactive web graphics via two approaches:

plot_ly() — native plotly syntax using ~ column references
ggplotly() — converts an existing ggplot2 object

Both produce charts with built-in zoom, pan, hover tooltips, and download buttons. plotly renders via JavaScript (plotly.js) rather than SVG, making it particularly suited for web dashboards.



1.7.1 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: plot_ly() Method
The tabset below shows a basic interactive plot created by using plot_ly():

The PlotThe Code Chunk












Show code
latest_pl <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

plot_ly(data = latest_pl,
        x    = ~Old,
        y    = ~Young)





The default output is already interactive: hover to see coordinates, drag to zoom, double-click to reset.






NoteWhat I Learned



plot_ly() produces a fully interactive chart with a single function call. The tilde syntax (~Old) is distinctive to plotly and signals a column reference, not a literal value. The default toolbar (zoom, pan, box select, lasso, download as PNG) is added automatically and requires no configuration — a stark contrast to the manual girafe() setup in ggiraph.





1.7.2 Working with Visual Variables: plot_ly() Method
The color argument maps a qualitative variable to colour. Clicking legend symbols toggles trace visibility:

The PlotThe Code Chunk












Show code
latest_pl <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

plot_ly(data   = latest_pl,
        x      = ~Old,
        y      = ~Young,
        color  = ~Continent,
        colors = viridis::viridis(length(unique(latest_pl$Continent))))





Click on the colour symbol at the legend to toggle groups on/off. Double-click to isolate one continent.






NoteWhat I Learned



Mapping color to a factor automatically creates a legend with toggle interactivity — a feature that would require custom JavaScript in a static chart. For demographic data, clicking continent labels to isolate groups is far more intuitive than filtering data and re-rendering. The colors argument accepts a palette function; viridis::viridis() generates the correct number of accessible colours automatically without requiring a named vector.





1.7.3 Creating an Interactive Scatter Plot: ggplotly() Method
ggplotly() converts any existing ggplot2 object into a plotly chart:

The PlotThe Code Chunk












Show code
latest_gg <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

p <- ggplot(data = latest_gg, aes(x = Old, y = Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p)





Notice that the only extra line needed is ggplotly(p). The zoom toolbar, hover tooltips, and legend toggles are added automatically.






NoteWhat I Learned



ggplotly() is the bridge between the ggplot2 ecosystem and plotly interactivity. When you already have carefully crafted ggplot code with proper themes, colour scales, and labels, ggplotly() preserves all of it while adding the full plotly toolbar. The trade-off: some advanced ggplot2 features may not translate perfectly. For standard scatter and bar charts, the conversion is reliable and fast.





1.7.4 Coordinated Multiple Views with plotly
The creation of a coordinated linked plot by using plotly involves three steps:

highlight_key() of plotly package is used as shared data
Two scatter plots are created by using ggplot2 functions
subplot() of plotly package is used to place them side by side


The PlotThe Code Chunk












Show code
latest_sub <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

d <- highlight_key(latest_sub)

p1 <- ggplot(data = d, aes(x = Old, y = Young)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#831843") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs % Young",
       x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(data = d, aes(x = Old, y = Population / 1e6)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7, colour = "#1e40af") +
  labs(title = "% Aged vs Population (M)",
       x = "% Aged", y = "Population (Millions)") +
  theme_minimal()

subplot(ggplotly(p1), ggplotly(p2))





Click on a data point of one scatter plot and see how the corresponding point on the other is selected.

highlight_key() simply creates an object of class crosstalk::SharedData. Visit this link to learn more about crosstalk.
subplot() places plotly charts side by side while maintaining the shared data linkage.







NoteWhat I Learned



highlight_key() is the glue between panels — it wraps a data frame with a SharedData object so plotly knows which rows correspond across charts. Without it, subplot() produces two fully independent charts. The pattern is always: shared data → two ggplots → two ggplotly() calls → one subplot(). This lets viewers ask multi-variable questions — “Is this highly-aged country also unusually large in population?” — answered with a single click.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
text:
1.8 Interactive Data Visualisation — crosstalk Methods






TipCore Concept



crosstalk is an add-on to the htmlwidgets package. It extends htmlwidgets with a set of classes, functions, and conventions for implementing cross-widget interactions — currently linked brushing and filtering. It works with plotly, DT, and leaflet.



1.8.1 Interactive Data Table: DT Package
The DT package is a wrapper of the JavaScript library DataTables. Data objects in R can be rendered as HTML tables using the JavaScript library DataTables (typically via R Markdown or Shiny):


Show code
DT::datatable(
  pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE)),
  class   = "compact",
  caption = paste("GlobalPopulation data — Year", max(pop_data$Year, na.rm = TRUE)),
  options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE)
)












NoteWhat I Learned



DT::datatable() is the fastest way to make any R data frame explorable in an HTML report. The search box — filtering hundreds of rows in real time — replaces multiple manual filter() calls. Tables and charts are complementary: charts show population-level patterns (clusters, trends), tables expose individual country-level values. This complementarity is exactly what linked brushing below exploits.





1.8.2 Linked Brushing: crosstalk Method

The PlotThe Code Chunk






















Show code
latest_brush <- pop_data %>% filter(Year == max(Year, na.rm = TRUE))

d <- highlight_key(latest_brush)

p <- ggplot(d, aes(Old, Young, colour = Continent)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +
  labs(x = "% Aged", y = "% Young") +
  theme_minimal()

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p), "plotly_selected")

crosstalk::bscols(
  gg,
  DT::datatable(d, options = list(pageLength = 8, scrollX = TRUE)),
  widths = 5
)





Click and drag to select points in the scatter plot — the table on the right filters to show only those rows.
Things to learn from the code:

highlight() is a plotly function that sets brushing options. "plotly_selected" specifies that selection is triggered by drag-select (lasso or box). These options are designed for linking multiple plotly graphs and may not behave as expected when linking to another htmlwidget via crosstalk.
bscols() is a crosstalk helper function that places HTML elements side by side using Bootstrap columns. It is designed for R Markdown / Quarto documents. Warning: This will bring in all of Bootstrap!
widths = 5 allocates 5/12 Bootstrap columns to each widget (plotly + DT), leaving 2 as margin.







ImportantWhat I Learned



Linked brushing with crosstalk combines three layers: plotly handles the selection event (plotly_selected), highlight_key() propagates the selection to all widgets sharing the same SharedData object, and DT reacts by filtering its rows. The result is a powerful explore-and-drill-down workflow: spot a demographic cluster in the chart (e.g., a group of highly-aged, low-young countries), drag to select it, and immediately read their exact country names and population figures in the table — all without a single line of JavaScript.
This is the highest-level interactivity achievable in a static Quarto HTML document without Shiny — and for many analytical tasks, it is sufficient.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a
section: 1.9 Comparison: ggiraph vs plotly vs crosstalk
text:
1.9 Comparison: ggiraph vs plotly vs crosstalk

ggiraphplotlycrosstalkDecision Guide


Best for:

Embedding interactive SVG charts in standalone HTML reports
Group-level hover highlighting via data_id
Coordinated multiple views within a single girafe() call
Full ggplot2 theme, scale and geom compatibility
Offline use (no external JS CDN required)

Key functions: geom_*_interactive(), girafe(), opts_tooltip(), opts_hover(), opts_hover_inv()


Best for:

Richer built-in toolbar (zoom, pan, box/lasso select, download PNG)
Interactive legends with click-to-toggle trace visibility
Coordinated views via highlight_key() + subplot()
Animation support (see Exercise 3b)
Web dashboards and Shiny integration

Key functions: plot_ly(), ggplotly(), highlight_key(), highlight(), subplot()


Best for:

Linking a chart to a sortable, searchable data table (DT)
Drill-down exploration: select visual cluster → read exact values
No Shiny server required — works in any HTML document

Key functions: highlight_key(), highlight(), bscols()











Criterion
ggiraph
plotly
crosstalk




Output format
SVG (inline HTML)
JS canvas
HTML layout


Tooltip customisation
Full CSS
Layout via text =
Via plotly


Group hover highlight
✅ data_id
Legend toggle
✅ via SharedData


Linked chart + table
❌
❌ alone
✅ DT


Animation support
❌
✅ frame =
❌


Shiny integration
Partial
Full
Partial


Ease for ggplot users
✅ drop-in
Medium
Medium












NoteOverall What I Learned



The key insight is that interactivity is not decoration — it is a layer of analysis that static charts cannot provide.

ggiraph is the most natural upgrade for ggplot2 users: swap geom_* → geom_*_interactive, add tooltip/data_id, wrap in girafe(). It is ideal when the goal is group-level highlighting across coordinated views within a report.
plotly excels when you need a richer toolbar or plan to extend to animations. ggplotly() bridges the ggplot2 workflow with minimal friction.
crosstalk enables the most powerful analytical workflow in a static document: drag-select a visual cluster, instantly read its members in a table — simulating a database query without any server code.

Choosing between them is a question of audience and context: ggiraph for polished reports, plotly for dashboards, crosstalk for exploratory analysis handoffs.

This exercise explores professional extensions to ggplot2 that enable publication-quality graphics. I’ll master label management, professional themes, and composite layouts while conducting deep statistical analysis of student performance patterns.
Building on fundamentals to create exceptional visualizations
Learning Objectives:

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
2.1 Package Installation

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse, knitr, GGally, viridis, ggridges, 
               scales, gridExtra, reshape2)

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
2.2 Data Import

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
4.1 Problem: Label Overlap Crisis


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#db2777") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = TRUE, color = "#831843", fill = "#fbcfe8") +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = 0.5, vjust = -0.5, size = 2.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,100), ylim = c(0,100)) +
  labs(title = "Label Overlap Problem: Unreadable IDs",
       x = "Mathematics Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()















WarningCritical Visualization Failure



With 322 data points, standard labeling creates complete overlap. The strong correlation (r = 0.831) is visible, but individual identification is impossible. This renders the visualization useless for student-level analysis.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
4.2 Solution: ggrepel Automatic Positioning


Show code
ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2, color = "#db2777") +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, se = TRUE, color = "#831843", fill = "#fbcfe8", size = 1.2) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold", size = 2.5,
                   box.padding = 0.35, point.padding = 0.5, max.overlaps = 15) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0,100), ylim = c(0,100)) +
  labs(title = "ggrepel Solution: Perfect Readability",
       subtitle = paste0("All ", n_students, " students identifiable | r = ", round(cor_maths_english, 3)),
       x = "Mathematics Score", y = "English Score") +
  theme_minimal()















TipIntelligent Positioning Algorithm



geom_label_repel() uses force-directed algorithms to: - Minimize label-label overlap - Maintain label-point connections - Optimize use of available space - Preserve data visualization integrity
Result: 100% readability with maintained correlation visualization.

  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )

  scale_color_gradient2(low = "#bfdbfe", mid = "#f9a8d4", high = "#ec4899",

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
In this hands-on exercise, I explored the Grammar of Graphics using ggplot2 to create meaningful visualizations. Through analyzing exam data from Primary 3 students, I discovered the power of layered graphics and data-driven insights.
Let’s visualize data beautifully
Learning Objectives: - Master basic principles and essential components of ggplot2 - Plot statistical graphics based on the principle of Layered Grammar of Graphics - Conduct data-driven analysis with dynamic insights - Extract meaningful patterns from visualizations
